card.intuition: A complete binary tree stored as an array where every parent is smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) than its children — the extreme element is always at index 0.
card.analogy: A hospital emergency room where each new patient is triaged and placed according to urgency. The most critical patient is always treated next, regardless of arrival order.
card.time_complexity: O(log n) push/pop; O(1) peek; O(n) build from array (heapify)
when_to_use: Always-get-the-minimum (or maximum) element efficiently: Dijkstra's, Prim's, A*
Merging k sorted lists — push first element of each list, pop min, push its successor
Scheduling tasks by priority, event-driven simulation
gotchas: Python's heapq is a min-heap only — for a max-heap, negate all values before pushing and negate again after popping
heapify() runs in O(n), not O(n log n) — building from an existing list is faster than n individual pushes
Heap does NOT support O(log n) arbitrary deletion — for that, use a lazy-deletion pattern (mark elements as deleted, skip them on pop)

exercise.prompt: Complete the min-heap sift-up operation used after inserting a new element at the end.
exercise.solution: heap[i], heap[parent]    # swap child with parent
hints: Swap the element at i with its parent to move it upward: heap[parent], heap[i] = heap[i], heap[parent]
After swapping, set i = parent and continue checking — the element may still need to bubble up further
exercise.test_code:
import heapq

h = []
heap_push(h, 5); heap_push(h, 3); heap_push(h, 8); heap_push(h, 1); heap_push(h, 4)
assert h[0] == 1, f'Min-heap root must be 1, got {h[0]}'

# Verify heap property: every parent <= its children
for i in range(1, len(h)):
    parent = (i-1)//2
    assert h[parent] <= h[i], f'Heap violated at i={i}: h[{parent}]={h[parent]} > h[{i}]={h[i]}'

# Compare with stdlib heapq
for v in [5,3,8,1,4]:
    import heapq
ref = [5,3,8,1,4]; heapq.heapify(ref)
assert sorted(h) == sorted(ref), 'Must contain same elements'
print('PASS')
